metadata.summary: Advanced CoT patterns beyond zero-shot: Tree of Thoughts for branching, Least-to-Most for sequential dependencies, and self-consistency for voting. Start with zero-shot CoT before escalating.

content.title: Chain-of-Thought Advanced Techniques
content.summary: Advanced reasoning patterns beyond zero-shot CoT: Tree of Thoughts (ToT) for branching decisions, Least-to-Most for sequential sub-dependencies, and Self-Consistency for high-stakes answers. Always start with zero-shot CoT ("Think step by step.") — it solves 70–80% of cases. Escalate to advanced techniques only when zero-shot fails.

why.p: Zero-shot CoT improves model accuracy on multi-step reasoning by 20–40% with zero token overhead beyond the trigger phrase. Advanced techniques (ToT, Least-to-Most) can further improve accuracy for specific problem structures but multiply API call count linearly — they should be used judiciously after zero-shot fails.

list.item: Multi-step reasoning where zero-shot CoT still produces wrong answers.
Branching solution paths (architecture choices, algorithm selection) — use Tree of Thoughts.
Sequential sub-dependencies (build order, migration path) — use Least-to-Most decomposition.
Verification pipelines where the model must self-check before returning.
Agent planning steps requiring reasoning about which tool to call next.
Simple factual lookups — CoT inflates token usage without improving accuracy.
Classification tasks with 3–5 clear categories — few-shot without CoT is cheaper.
High-throughput pipelines where latency matters — each ToT branch is a separate API call.
When self-consistency requires 5–10 samples — cost multiplies linearly; benchmark gain vs. cost first.

section.name: tree-of-thoughts
section.p: Tree of Thoughts explores multiple reasoning paths in parallel by generating branches, scoring them, and pruning low-value paths. It is effective for problems with multiple solution strategies (e.g., choosing between architectures) but requires careful cost control.

rule.statement: Set a hard max_depth of 2–3 levels in Tree of Thoughts; with num_branches=3 and max_depth=3 you make up to 9+ API calls per problem — cost explodes without a depth cap.
rule.rationale: ToT is not bounded by default. A 3-branch, 3-level tree makes at least 9 evaluation calls. In a production agent, this can stall a pipeline for minutes and exhaust rate limits.

rule.statement: Use response_format={"type": "json_object"} for thought evaluation in ToT; assign score=0 on JSON parse failure rather than raising an exception.
rule.rationale: Models occasionally produce malformed JSON under structured reasoning load. A crashed evaluation kills the entire ToT run; score=0 gracefully eliminates the path.

rule.statement: Set a wall-clock timeout on ToT runs in agentic pipelines — slow API responses with 9+ branches can stall the pipeline for minutes without a timeout.
rule.rationale: ToT with slow API responses is unbounded in wall time. An agent pool that blocks on one long ToT run starves other tasks.

example.title: ToT evaluation with graceful degradation

section.name: least-to-most-decomposition
section.p: Least-to-Most breaks complex problems into simpler sub-problems, solves them in order from simplest to hardest, and uses earlier solutions as context for later ones. It is effective for sequential dependency chains (build systems, migrations, multi-stage decision trees).

rule.statement: Cache the decomposition step output in Least-to-Most — if the LLM already decomposed the problem, do not re-decompose on retry; only retry the failed subproblem solution.
rule.rationale: Decomposition calls are expensive. If only one subproblem solution fails, re-running decomposition wastes tokens and risks getting a different (possibly worse) decomposition.

antipattern.description: Including the full CoT reasoning trace in the next turn's context in a multi-turn agent.
antipattern.reason: CoT traces bloat the context window rapidly. A 5-turn conversation with full traces can exceed 50K tokens. Strip or summarize traces between turns; the parent agent needs only the final answer.

section.name: strategy-selection
section.p: Selecting the right CoT strategy depends on problem structure. A heuristic based on keywords and length can guide the initial choice; always benchmark before hardcoding.

rule.statement: Always try zero-shot CoT ("Think step by step.") first — it solves 70–80% of cases at the cost of only a few extra tokens. Escalate to advanced techniques only after zero-shot fails on your specific problem class.
rule.rationale: Advanced techniques multiply API calls, cost, and latency. Zero-shot is free in terms of additional API calls. Measure before upgrading strategy.

rule.statement: Use o1/o3 (OpenAI) or Claude extended thinking (Anthropic) for problems that natively benefit from deep reasoning — they handle CoT internally and are cheaper than explicit multi-call ToT.
rule.rationale: Extended thinking tokens are billed but not visible in the response text — they represent internal reasoning the model does natively. Running explicit ToT on top of o1 is redundant and expensive.

section.p: Strategy heuristic: inspect problem keywords for branching signals (choose, compare, which, options, alternative, versus, tradeoff) and sequential signals (step, before, depends, order, first, then, sequence, pipeline, migration). Long problems (>500 chars) default to Least-to-Most.

section.name: self-consistency
section.p: Self-consistency runs N parallel reasoning paths and votes on the majority answer. It improves accuracy when reasoning paths are diverse and independent.

rule.statement: For self-consistency: sample at temperature=0.7, deduplicate identical outputs before majority vote, and benchmark accuracy gain vs. cost before using more than 5 samples.
rule.rationale: Low-temperature sampling produces identical outputs that artificially inflate one answer's count. Cost multiplies linearly with sample count — 10 samples = 10x the cost of a single call.

antipattern.description: Using self-consistency when all samples share the same systematic bias.
antipattern.reason: If the model has a consistent bias on a problem type, majority vote amplifies the wrong answer. Self-consistency assumes independent errors; correlated errors defeat it.

list.item: Tree of Thoughts with num_branches=3, max_depth=3 makes up to 9+ API calls per problem — cost explodes for complex trees.
Self-consistency relies on majority vote; if all samples share the same bias, the wrong answer wins consistently.
Least-to-Most requires the LLM to decompose correctly — if decomposition is wrong, all downstream steps are wrong.
Models can produce confident, well-structured reasoning that is factually incorrect ("hallucination with steps").
JSON response format is required for thought evaluation; models occasionally produce malformed JSON under the structured reasoning load.
An agent running ToT must set a wall-clock timeout — a 9-branch tree with slow API responses can stall the pipeline for minutes.
JSON parse failures in thought evaluation are common — always wrap in try/except and assign score=0 on parse failure rather than crashing.
Self-consistency samples must be deduplicated before majority vote — identical outputs (low temperature) artificially inflate one answer's count.
Extended Thinking (Claude) and o1 reasoning tokens are billed but not visible in the response text — account for them in cost tracking.
Do NOT include the full CoT trace in the next turn's context — it bloats the context window rapidly in multi-turn agents; summarize or strip it.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Advanced CoT techniques are best encapsulated inside a reasoning subagent that exposes a single reasoning(problem, strategy) interface. The parent agent passes a problem description and a strategy hint (zero_shot / tree_of_thoughts / least_to_most); the reasoning subagent selects the implementation, runs it, and returns a structured result with the reasoning trace and final answer. The parent agent uses only the final answer, not the trace — this keeps context clean across multi-turn conversations.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — can invoke a CoT reasoning step as part of design/spec generation.
A dedicated reasoning-agent wrapping ChainOfThoughtService for complex decision tasks.
Zero-shot trigger (cheapest, try first): "Think step by step. Show your reasoning before giving the final answer."
Tree of Thoughts trigger for branching: "Generate 3 distinct approaches to this problem, evaluate each on relevance and soundness (score 1-10), then choose the best and complete it."
Least-to-Most for sequential: "First list the subproblems from simplest to hardest. Then solve each in order, using previous solutions as context."
Self-Consistency (sampling loop): "Think step by step and give your final answer." (sample N times at temp=0.7, then take majority vote)

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: CLI and framework tools for CoT experimentation:

list.item: openai / anthropic (Python SDK) — LLM calls for CoT steps. Install: pip install openai anthropic
tenacity — Retry on JSON parse failure from CoT output. Install: pip install tenacity
dspy — Framework for programmatic CoT prompt optimization. Install: pip install dspy-ai. Docs: dspy.ai
guidance — Constrained generation for structured CoT. Install: pip install guidance. Docs: github.com/guidance-ai/guidance
OpenAI GPT-4o / o1 — SaaS. o1 has native CoT reasoning built in.
Anthropic Claude (extended thinking) — SaaS. Extended thinking = native CoT; see claude-advanced-features.
LangSmith — SaaS. Trace and visualize CoT reasoning chains.
DSPy — OSS. Programmatically optimize CoT prompts.
Weights & Biases — SaaS. Log reasoning traces and evaluate accuracy.
Always try zero-shot CoT first ("Think step by step.") — it solves 70–80% of cases at minimal cost.
Use response_format={"type": "json_object"} for thought evaluation in ToT to prevent parse failures.
Set a hard max_depth on Tree of Thoughts (2–3 levels max) to prevent runaway API call chains.
For self-consistency, sample at temperature=0.7 and use the most common answer, not the first.
Cache the decomposition step output in Least-to-Most — if the LLM already decomposed the problem, don't re-decompose on retry.
Use o1 / o3 models for problems that natively benefit from extended reasoning — they handle CoT internally and are cheaper than explicit multi-call ToT.

template.purpose: Zero-shot, ToT, and Least-to-Most trigger phrases
